meta.title: Workplace-Violence Prevention Program — Emergency Department
meta.subtitle: WPV program (OSHA 3148 five elements / OSH Act §5(a)(1) / Cal-OSHA 8 CCR 3342)
meta.author: HSE Healthcare
meta.deid_notice: All personnel, patients, and visitors are referred to by role label. No victim, assailant, or known-risk patient is named, and no behavioural-health flag is tied to a person; any known-risk flagging is a de-identified clinical mechanism held confidentially and separately. The WPV incident log below is de-identified and aggregated, with categories of fewer than five suppressed. Identifiers were pseudonymised before drafting and the re-identification key is held separately, not part of this document.

sections.text: Emergency department — evening / overnight reception & triage. Violence types in scope: type 2 (patient / visitor — the dominant healthcare type) and type 1 (criminal-intent at reception). The worksite hazard analysis (below) was recorded BEFORE any control. Any named victim, assailant, or known-risk patient and any behavioural-health flag were scrubbed to role level before drafting.
Violence-type taxonomy (type 1-4) — each type drives its control set

rows: No legitimate relationship to the workplace
Triage sightlines, staffing/skill-mix, de-identified flagging, de-escalation
sections.caption: OSHA / NIOSH / Cal-OSHA type-1-4 violence taxonomy (KB-REG-WPV-OSHA3148)

sections.text: Worksite hazard analysis (de-identified / aggregated — OSHA 3148 element 2)

The worksite analysis is recorded FIRST and is the basis of every control. It draws on the de-identified records / incident review, a walkthrough, and a staff survey — never a named victim or assailant. A program with no worksite analysis fails.

rows: Open desk, no barrier, poor sightlines to waiting area; no duress alarm
Crowding + long waits driving verbal aggression; no de-escalation prompts
No line-of-sight, no personal duress, single-staff assessment
sections.caption: De-identified, aggregated worksite analysis — no victim/assailant/known-risk patient named; small populations suppressed

sections.text: Hierarchy-ranked controls (worksite analysis → environmental → administrative → reactive last)

Environmental-and-administrative-first: the exposure is designed out by controlled access, sightlines, alarms/duress, and staffing BEFORE any reactive measure. A 'issue personal alarms / run self-defence training' program with no environmental or administrative control is a FLAG pushed up the hierarchy — de-escalation, response training, and personal alarms are the documented last lines, never the headline control.

rows.control: Substitute the exposure where possible — relocate uncontrolled external access; remove the open ambulance-bay route after hours
Environmental: controlled access & egress, reception barrier + improved triage sightlines, duress/panic alarms, CCTV, waiting-area / queue redesign
Administrative: staffing & skill-mix at peak, lone-working procedure for assessment rooms + late-shift egress, de-identified known-risk-patient flagging, no-retaliation reporting
De-escalation & response training, response team, post-incident support, and personal alarms — the residual last line

sections.text: Reactive measures are NOT the primary control. A program whose headline control is 'personal alarms + self-defence training' with no access-control, sightline, alarm-system, or staffing/lone-working control is a FLAG pushed up the hierarchy.

items: De-escalation procedure — recognition of escalation cues, verbal de-escalation, environmental separation; trained to all front-of-house staff.
Response protocol — duress activation → response team → security escalation → safe withdrawal; rehearsed.
Post-incident support — confidential debrief, occupational-health / EAP referral, no-retaliation, return-to-work support.
Training plan — WPV awareness, type-1-4 recognition, de-escalation, reporting; refreshed annually with employee involvement (OSHA 3148 element 4).

sections.text: Confidential WPV incident log structure (de-identified / aggregated — OSHA 3148 element 5 / Cal-OSHA 8 CCR 3342)

sections.caption: Structural / aggregated log only — never line-level identified; categories of fewer than five suppressed so a suppressed cell cannot be back-calculated from totals

rows.title: Reactive-led control proposed where the exposure could be designed out
rows.description: A draft program headlined 'issue personal alarms + run self-defence training' was flagged and pushed up the hierarchy — the exposure is first designed out by controlled access, triage sightlines, duress alarms, and staffing.

rows.title: No worksite hazard analysis underpinning the original draft
rows.description: The original draft proposed controls with no worksite hazard analysis; a de-identified records/walkthrough/survey analysis was authored as the basis of every control (OSHA 3148 element 2).

sections.text: After substitution (controlled external access), environmental controls (reception barrier, triage sightlines, duress alarms, CCTV, queue redesign), and administrative controls (peak staffing, lone-working procedure, de-identified flagging), the residual violence risk is framed via risk_matrix as Low-Medium, with de-escalation / response training and personal alarms as the last line.

rows.action: Install the reception barrier + duress alarms and improve triage sightlines
Implement the lone-working procedure for assessment rooms + late-shift egress
Deliver de-escalation & response training with employee involvement; stand up the confidential WPV incident log

items: WPV incident counts taken from the de-identified, aggregated incident log; categories of fewer than five are suppressed — a missing figure is a [GAP], never invented.
India site: state occupational-safety return owed — [GAP] (resolve the state via hse-india; no national form number minted).

OSHA Publication 3148 (2016) — the five program elements (management commitment & worker participation, worksite analysis, hazard prevention & control, training, recordkeeping & evaluation), enforced under the OSH Act §5(a)(1) General Duty Clause where a recognized WPV hazard exists
Cal/OSHA Workplace Violence Prevention in Health Care, 8 CCR 3342 — the binding standard in California (written plan, type-1-4 taxonomy, employee involvement, violent-incident log)
UK HSE management of work-related violence guidance + NICE NG10 (violence & aggression: short-term management) for the UK leg
India occupational-safety provisions via hse-india (no national form number)
ISO 45001 §6.1.2 — hazard identification & risk assessment (with the ISO 45003 psychosocial link for the violence aftermath)
Review trigger: annual program review / on-incident / on-service-change — whichever is first
Decision-support only — a competent person (healthcare-security / WPV / occupational-health professional) must review before use.
